title: Finite-size general security for differential phase shift keying via variable-length quantum key distribution
abstract: Differential phase shift keying (DPSK) constitutes a pathway towards practical quantum key distribution by using affordable commercial technologies, and robust theoretical foundations. Recent advances in the security of DPSK have proven its security against general adversaries, albeit requiring limitations, including strong repetition rate constraints at the security proof and costly statistical estimators. In this work, we overcome said limitations by leveraging recent techniques in variable-length general security by using entropy accumulation techniques based on Rényi leftover hashing, together with conic optimization methods. Our approach achieves secret key rates with $10^5$ signals beyond 12 dB, constituting a robust proof of the experimental implementability of industrial-grade DPSK.
summary: This paper provides a new security proof for DPSK quantum key distribution that works with much smaller data blocks than previously possible. By using advanced entropy accumulation and conic optimization, it proves that industrial-grade, practical QKD is achievable even with significant channel loss and experimental noise.
main_problem: Achieving finite-size general security for Differential Phase Shift Keying (DPSK) QKD without requiring extremely large block sizes or low repetition rates.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that DPSK can achieve secure key rates with block sizes as small as 10^5 signals at losses beyond 12 dB, significantly outperforming previous benchmarks.
method: The study uses the Marginal-Constrained Entropy Accumulation Theorem (MEAT) and Rényi leftover hashing, formulated as a conic optimization problem using the Julia programming language.
model_or_system: A DPSK QKD protocol where Alice sends coherent states and Bob performs threshold detection using a Mach-Zehnder interferometer, incorporating channel loss, dark counts, and modulation asymmetry.
key_observables: Secret key rate (bits per pulse), detection outcomes (clicks, double-clicks, no-clicks), and channel loss (dB).
important_parameters: Block size (n) ranging from 10^5 to 10^9, channel loss (up to 30 dB), dark count probability (pd), and asymmetry factor (gamma).
main_assumptions: The security relies on a no-signaling condition between quantum signals and assumes Eve is a general adversary.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows secret key rates vs. channel loss for various block sizes; Figure 2 shows the impact of dark count rates; Figure 3 shows the dependence on the Rényi parameter; Figure 4 shows the impact of modulation asymmetry.
paper_structure: The paper defines the DPSK protocol, establishes an operational equivalence to a simpler relativistic protocol, applies the MEAT framework with dimension reduction via squashing maps, and performs numerical optimization to derive key rates.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum communications as it provides a rigorous security foundation for practical, high-rate, and low-complexity photonic QKD implementations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Secret key generation rates for DPSK model under different block sizes n, fEC = 1.10, ξ = 0.005 and pd = 0. All other parameters were optimized for each point of the plot.
Figure 2: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under different dark count rates pd, fEC = 1.10, ξ = 0.005 and n = 108. All other parameters were optimized for each point of the plot.

Figure 3: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under values for the R´enyi parameter α, pd = 0, fEC = 1.10, χ = 10 dB, ξ = 0.005, pK = 0.96 and diverse block sizes n. The amplitude of the coherent states was set to β = 0.45.

Figure 4: Secret key generation rates for DPSK under different asymmetry factors γ, coherent state amplitude β = 0.45, pd = 10−5, fEC = 1.10, ξ = 0.005 and n = 107. The R´enyi parameter α and pK were optimized for each point of the plot.
